coredata.dc:title: Electron field emission from diamond-like carbon nanodot arrays
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2009 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, C.
Yan, P.X.
Li, X.C.
Chong, E.M.
coredata.dc:description: Diamond-like carbon (DLC) nanodot arrays were prepared by filtered cathodic arc plasma (FCAP) technique at room temperature with the aid of anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) template at a bias of −50V. The average diameter of highly ordered and vertical DLC nanodots was ∼90nm, and the nanodot density was estimated to be ∼1010 cm−2. A broad asymmetric band attributed to characteristic band of DLC ranging from 1000 to 2000cm−1 was detected by Raman spectrum. A sp3/(sp3+sp2) ratio of 68.2% was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS). A low threshold field of 4V/μm at 10mA/cm2 and a high current density of 43.2mA/cm2 at 5V/μm were achieved. And based on Fowler–Nordheim plot, the value of work function for the DLC nanodot arrays was estimated in the range 0.4–1.84 from a linear plot. The results show that the DLC nanodot arrays can be a promising candidate as a field emission emitter.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Field emission
DLC nanodot arrays
Filtered cathodic arc plasma
AAO template

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of fabricating DLC nanodot arrays: (a) AAO template before deposition of DLC; (b) after deposition of DLC by FCAP; (c) after removing of the AAO template.
FESEM images: (a) AAO template before deposition; (b) DLC layer deposited at 0V bias; (c and d) high and low magnification images of DLC nanodot arrays deposited at −50V bias.
Raman spectrum and the resulting fitted curve of the DLC nanodot arrays deposited at −50V bias.
XPS spectrum of C 1s peak for the DLC nanodot arrays deposited at −50V bias, with its decomposition into the sp2 (at 284.4eV), sp3 (at 285.2eV) and C–O (at 286.5eV) peaks.
Current density (J) as a function of electric field (E) for the DLC nanodot arrays, and a Fowler–Nordheim plot is included.

head.ce:title: Electron field emission from diamond-like carbon nanodot arrays

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diamond-like carbon (DLC) nanodot arrays were prepared by filtered cathodic arc plasma (FCAP) technique at room temperature with the aid of anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) template at a bias of −50V. The average diameter of highly ordered and vertical DLC nanodots was ∼90nm, and the nanodot density was estimated to be ∼1010 cm−2. A broad asymmetric band attributed to characteristic band of DLC ranging from 1000 to 2000cm−1 was detected by Raman spectrum. A sp3/(sp3+sp2) ratio of 68.2% was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS). A low threshold field of 4V/μm at 10mA/cm2 and a high current density of 43.2mA/cm2 at 5V/μm were achieved. And based on Fowler–Nordheim plot, the value of work function for the DLC nanodot arrays was estimated in the range 0.4–1.84 from a linear plot. The results show that the DLC nanodot arrays can be a promising candidate as a field emission emitter.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decade, a variety of field emission materials have been studied extensively [1–9], due to their widespread applications, such as in flat panel displays, vacuum microelectronic devices and vacuum nanoelectronic devices. Among them, the carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are recognized as one kind of excellent carbon-based material for field emission. [10–13]. However, the searching for the better field emitter is still a highly focused research area. The interest in DLC materials has been growing because they possess many excellent physical and chemical properties, for example, high mechanical hardness, chemical inertness, optical transparency, wide band gap and low electron affinity [14]. Among them, low electron affinity is very important for this application, because the electrons can be easily emitted into vacuum at a low electron affinity. In addition, high-current emission at a relatively low field is also much attractive for the FE applications. And, significant effort is being devoted to reducing the tip size and increasing the density of the emitting sites. Therefore, the synthesis of DLC nanodot arrays with controllable size and density is an important topic worthy of exploration.

In this article, uniform and patterned DLC nanodot arrays of large area are prepared on AAO templates by FCAP technique with a bias of −50V. The influence of negative bias on the growth of DLC nanodot arrays is discussed preliminarily and the field emission behaviors are investigated.

The equipment of the filtered cathodic arc plasma with 60° bend duct to deposit the DLC nanodot arrays was made by us. The main principle can refer to Ref. [15]. The AAO templates used as substrates were prepared by two-step anodization, which has been reported in detail elsewhere [16]. Before deposition, the AAO templates, as shown in Fig. 1(a), were cleaned ultrasonically using acetone and alcohol sequentially, and then were blown dry with argon gas. Finally they were fixed on the samples support in vacuum chamber. The carbon plasma was generated by a cathodic arc plasma source made of a high pure graphite cathode (>99.99%). The deposition power was 2000W. The filtered magnetic current was 30A. The base pressure was 7.5×10−6 Torr. Argon (purity 99.99%) was used as the protective gas, and the gas flow rate was kept constant at 1.5sccm. The 0 and −50V bias were applied to the substrate, respectively. The deposition time was 120min, and the deposition was carried out at room temperature. In the process, DLC can be deposited layer by layer into the AAO nanopores. After deposition, the DLC film would fill in the AAO nanopores and cover its surface, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Then the template was removed off dissolving in HgCl2 and NaOH solutions, respectively. Finally, the DLC nanodot arrays were obtained, as seen from Fig. 1(c).

A field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Hitachi S-4800) was used to characterize the morphology of the DLC nanodot arrays. Raman spectrum was acquired on a microRaman system of JOBIN-IVON LabRam HR800 with a laser source wavelength of 535nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using Perkin-Elmer PHI-5702 equipped with a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source(photon energy 1476.6eV) to determine the surface composition and chemical states. The spectrometer was calibrated by Au4f7/2 (binding energy of 84eV) with respect to the Fermi level. The field emission properties were measured at 10−6 Torr in a parallel plate configuration at room temperature. The testing cathode area was 3.14mm2. The distance between the specimen and the anode was about 40μm, which was controlled by a precise screwmeter.

With the aid of FCAP and AAO templates, highly ordered and vertical DLC nanodot arrays were prepared. The FESEM image of the as-prepared AAO substrate was shown in Fig. 2(a). As seen in Fig. 2(b), no DLC nanodot arrays were obtained when the substrate bias was 0V. With the substrate bias of −50V, DLC can be deposited layer by layer into the AAO nanopores. After the template was removed off, the DLC nanodot arrays were obtained. The diameter of DLC nanodots was ∼90nm and the nanodot density was estimated to be ∼1010 cm−2, which can be seen in Fig. 2(c) and (d). Therefore, it can be inferred that the growth of the DLC nanodot arrays strongly depends on the substrate bias. The application of negative bias to the substrate will influence the energy of carbon ions arriving at the substrate [17]. At 0V bias, the carbon ions arriving at the substrate have lower deposition energy, which makes the carbon ions grow on the skeleton construction of the AAO template. It will only result in a thin layer gradually covering the AAO surface but without the formation of nanodot arrays. With the substrate bias of −50V, the carbon ions arriving at the substrate have higher deposition energy which enhances the diffusion of growth species on the AAO template surface. So they are easy to enter into the nanopores of AAO template, which induces the forming of DLC nanodot arrays.

Raman spectroscopy is a common and powerful tool to study the structural properties of DLC due to the high scattering efficiency of the carbon–carbon bonding states. The Raman spectra acquired between 1000 and 2000cm−1 from the DLC nanodot arrays. It reveals a typical DLC structure characterized by one band composed of G peak at around 1551cm−1and D peak at around 1354cm−1, as shown in Fig. 3. Both D and G peaks are typical features of highly disordered graphite-like structures. The G band corresponds to the symmetric E2g C–C stretching mode in graphite-like materials, while the D band is attributed to bond angle disorder in the graphite-like microdomains affected by sp3 bonds [18].

Raman spectrum is relatively unreliable in quantitative analysis of DLC. Compared with this, XPS is one of the standard methods in the determination of the sp2 and the sp3 fractions in DLC materials on an absolute level. The bonding type (sp3, sp2) of DLC nanodot arrays is determined by XPS in the binding energy region from 275 to 300eV. C 1s peak and the deconvolution of XPS spectrum is displayed in Fig. 4. The C 1s peak is fitted by a Gaussian function with three components centered at 284.4±0.1eV (FWHM=1.2±0.05eV), 285.2±0.1eV (FWHM=1.2±0.05eV) and 286.5±0.2eV (FWHM=1.4±0.05eV), respectively. And these components are corresponding to sp2 bonded carbon, sp3 bonded carbon and C–O bonds on the surface due to contamination by air exposure [19], respectively. Since the area of each peak is directly related to the concentration of the corresponding phase, the sp3 content was estimated by taking the ratio of sp3 peak area over the sum of sp3, sp2 peak and C–O peak area. As a result, it is found that the fraction of sp3 bonded carbon atoms [sp3/(sp3+sp2)] is about 68.2% for the DLC nanodot arrays.

Fig. 5 shows the current density versus electric field (J–E) characteristic of the DLC nanodot arrays. The threshold field of 4V/μm at 10mA/cm2 and a high current density of 43.2mA/cm2 at 5V/μm are achieved. In general, field emission current depends on the geometry and the work function of the sample [20]. It is known that the field intensity can be greatly enhanced by sharpening the tips of the emitter. Therefore, the nano-sized tips of the DLC nanodot arrays can boost up the field intensity, which may reduce threshold field strength. And a famous model, the FN equation, is used here to identify the work function of the DLC nanodot arrays. According to the FN model, field emission current from semiconductor is attributed to the tunneling of electrons from the material into vacuum under the influence of an electric field [21]. The FN equation [22] can be express as
(1)
J =
E
loc
2
exp ( ‐ 6.8 ×
10
7
Φ
3 / 2
/
E
loc
)
where J is the electron current density at the emitting top, E loc is the local electric field, and Φ is the work function of the DLC nanodot arrays. For an isolated hemisphere,
(2)
E
loc
= V / α
R
top
where V is the applied voltage, R top is the top radius of curvature, and α is a modifying factor. According to Eqs. (1) and (2), it could be obtained
(3)
ln ( I /
V
2
) = 1 / V ( ‐ 6.8 ×
10
7
α
R
top
Φ
3 / 2
) + offset
αR top Φ 3⧸2 can be evaluated from the slope coefficient of the F–N plot of ln(I/V 2) against (1/V), as shown in the inset of Fig. 5. The value of α in the range of 1–10 has been applied in previous study [23]. Using α of 1–10, with R top=45nm for our calculations, the work functions of the DLC nanodot arrays are calculated in the range 0.4–1.84. The results show that the DLC nanodot arrays can be a promising candidate as a field emission emitter.

DLC nanodot arrays were fabricated by FCAP technique at room temperature. At a fixed power and Ar flow rate, the growth of the DLC nanodot arrays strongly depends on the substrate bias. A broad asymmetric band detected by Raman spectrum was the characteristic band of DLC. And the XPS results indicated that the fraction of sp3 bonded carbon atoms was 68.2% for the DLC nanodot arrays. The DLC nanodot arrays exhibited fairly good field emission properties. A low threshold field of 4V/μm at 10mA/cm2 and a high current density of 43.2mA/cm2 at 5V/μm were achieved. According to the FN model, the work function of the DLC nanodot arrays was estimated in the range of 0.4–1.84 from the linearity plot. Hence the DLC nanodot arrays deserve more attention in the search of excellent field emitters.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the fund from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 60907007).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proceedings of the Royal Society of London, Series A
